macro_segments.title: Post-dating and accurate dating of sale documents (שטרי מכר) — principles and limitations

micro_segments.title: The rule: if you know the date of the kinyan, write it; if not, write today's date to avoid appearing false (מחזי כשקרא)
Why sales of land with achrayus can be post-dated: liens exist from the time of sale even without a document, and buyers are presumed to know this
Contrast: loans cannot be post-dated because liens only come into being when the שטר is written, making a post-dated loan document invalid (שטר מוקדם)
Writing both the place where the witnesses are located and the place where the event occurred, citing Rashbam's interpretation

macro_segments.title: The שובר (receipt) and the פגום שטר (defective document) — protecting both creditor and debtor through document structure

micro_segments.title: Why we do not convert a 100-unit שטר into two 50-unit שטרות: mutual benefits for both malveh (leverage) and loveh (avoidance of שבועה requirement)
Why we do not convert two 50-unit שטרות into a single 100-unit שטר: preserves distinct advantages for each party
The fraud scenario: preventing a creditor from reducing a 100-unit loan to 50 units by claiming the debtor already paid, potentially obscuring a genuine receipt

macro_segments.title: Same-name problem in document identification: two people named Yosef ben Shimon in the same city

micro_segments.title: The basic rule: neither party can bring a חוב שטר (debt document) against the other; neither can a third party bring a debt document against both
Solution: use three generations of names (גבהון = triple patronymic); if still ambiguous, use identifying characteristics (סימנים); if none suffice, note priestly or levitical status (כהן, לוי)

macro_segments.title: The open-ended 'from you' in a document: does it function as a bearer bond or require identification of the actual creditor?

micro_segments.title: Rav Huna's challenge: if a שטר says 'I borrowed from you' but uses the generic 'you,' can we know who the creditor really is?
Proof from גט without a date: Abba Shaul says that 'today' in a גט makes it dated because of עדי מסירה (witnesses to delivery), not because today itself has special force
Tosafot's alternative: 'from you' might be interpreted as a bearer document—whoever holds it is owed the debt, similar to how 'today' in a גט might mean 'whenever presented to beit din'
Rava's position: we do not fear that the document was dropped and found by someone else; we assume it belongs to whoever is presenting it
Abaye's distinction: we do not fear one person dropping and one person finding it (נפילה דחד), but we do fear loss among many or ambiguity when a generic term is used (נפילה דרבים)

A Baraita limits collection on a document with one's own name if another person of the same name exists: the same logic that prevents mutual collection between identically named people applies outward

The debate hinges on whether the document itself is acquired through delivery (אותיות נקנות במסירה): if yes, delivery of the document transfers the debt; if no, one cannot be sure the document is actually theirs

topical_tags.term: שם אחד יוסף בן שמעון (same-name problem)
אותיות נקנות במסירה (acquisition of document by delivery)
